theorems.formal_statement: forall (n : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), Circle.rot n 0 x = x
theorems.informal_statement: Rotating by zero leaves every node unchanged.
theorems.name: Rotation zero
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Rotation.lean.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n a b : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), Circle.rot n a (Circle.rot n b x) = Circle.rot n (a+b) x
theorems.informal_statement: Composing rotations adds their strides.
theorems.name: Rotation composition
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Rotation.lean.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n a : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), x + (a : ZMod n) - (a : ZMod n) = x
theorems.informal_statement: A rotation followed by the opposite rotation returns to the starting node.
theorems.name: Rotation inverse
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Rotation.lean.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n stride start steps : Nat), Circle.closesAt n stride start steps iff ((steps * stride : Nat) : ZMod n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: A coil closes at step m exactly when m times its stride is zero in C_n.
theorems.name: Closure condition
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Coil.lean.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.period n k = n / Nat.gcd n k
theorems.informal_statement: The period of stride k on C_n is n/gcd(n,k).
theorems.name: Period equals n divided by gcd
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Period.lean using ZMod.addOrderOf_coe.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.orbitClassCount n k = Nat.gcd n k
theorems.informal_statement: Stride k partitions C_n into gcd(n,k) disjoint cycles.
theorems.name: Orbit decomposition count
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Orbit.lean using the quotient by the stride-generated additive subgroup, Lagrange's theorem, Fintype.card_zmultiples, and ZMod.addOrderOf_coe.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {p k : Nat}, Nat.Prime p -> 0 < k -> k < p -> Circle.period p k = p
theorems.informal_statement: Every nonzero stride smaller than a prime circle size generates a full coil.
theorems.name: Prime full coil
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Prime.lean from CC-T0005 and Nat.coprime_of_lt_prime.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n k : Nat), Function.Bijective (Circle.scale n k) iff Nat.Coprime n k
theorems.informal_statement: Scaling by k is invertible on C_n exactly when gcd(n,k)=1.
theorems.name: Scaling invertible iff coprime
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean using ZMod.isUnit_iff_coprime and IsUnit.isUnit_iff_mulLeft_bijective.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus value winding residue : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> residue < modulus -> winding * modulus + residue = value -> winding = Circle.liftWinding modulus value and residue = Circle.liftResidue modulus value
theorems.informal_statement: Every natural number has a unique winding/residue decomposition.
theorems.name: Lift unique
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean using natural-number division and modulo uniqueness.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus left right : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> Circle.liftAddWinding modulus left right * modulus + Circle.liftAddResidue modulus left right = left + right and Circle.liftAddResidue modulus left right < modulus
theorems.informal_statement: Lifted addition concatenates two path counts by adding windings, adding residues, and carrying residue overflow into the winding count.
theorems.name: Addition as path concatenation
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean using quotient/remainder decomposition of the sum of residues.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus value : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> Circle.liftWinding modulus value * modulus + Circle.liftResidue modulus value = value and Circle.liftResidue modulus value < modulus
theorems.informal_statement: Every natural number has a canonical winding/residue decomposition for each positive modulus.
theorems.name: Lift exists
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean from Nat.div_add_mod and Nat.mod_lt.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus value : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> Circle.liftSuccWinding modulus value * modulus + Circle.liftSuccResidue modulus value = value + 1 and Circle.liftSuccResidue modulus value < modulus
theorems.informal_statement: Successor is unit residue motion with carry into the winding count.
theorems.name: Lift successor with carry
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean using quotient/remainder decomposition of residue+1.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus value : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> Circle.liftAddWinding modulus value 0 = Circle.liftWinding modulus value and Circle.liftAddResidue modulus value 0 = Circle.liftResidue modulus value
theorems.informal_statement: Zero is a right identity for lifted addition.
theorems.name: Lifted addition right zero identity
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus left middle right : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> value((left+middle)+right as a lifted addition) = value(left+(middle+right) as a lifted addition)
theorems.informal_statement: Lifted addition is associative at the reconstructed-value level.
theorems.name: Lifted addition associativity
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean by reducing both lifted additions to natural-number sums.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus value : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> Circle.liftAddWinding modulus 0 value = Circle.liftWinding modulus value and Circle.liftAddResidue modulus 0 value = Circle.liftResidue modulus value
theorems.informal_statement: Zero is a left identity for lifted addition.
theorems.name: Lifted addition left zero identity
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {modulus winding residue value steps : Nat}, modulus != 0 -> residue < modulus -> winding * modulus + residue = value -> iterating the lifted successor transition steps times reconstructs value + steps and leaves residue bounded
theorems.informal_statement: Repeated lifted successor corresponds to natural-number induction by unit steps.
theorems.name: Iterated lifted successor
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Winding.lean by induction on the number of successor steps.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), Circle.scale n 1 x = x
theorems.informal_statement: Scaling by one leaves every node unchanged.
theorems.name: Scaling by one identity
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by reducing scale-by-one to one multiplication in ZMod.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n a b : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), Circle.scale n a (Circle.scale n b x) = Circle.scale n (a*b) x
theorems.informal_statement: Composing scaling maps multiplies their scale factors.
theorems.name: Scaling composition
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from associativity and natural-number multiplication casting into ZMod.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n k stride : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), Circle.scale n k (Circle.rot n stride x) = Circle.rot n (k*stride) (Circle.scale n k x)
theorems.informal_statement: Scaling a rotated node equals rotating the scaled node by the scaled stride.
theorems.name: Scaling transports rotation stride
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by distributivity of multiplication over addition in ZMod.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n k stride start steps : Nat), Circle.scale n k (Circle.coilStep n stride start steps) = Circle.coilStep n (k*stride) (k*start) steps
theorems.informal_statement: Scaling a finite coil step gives the matching coil step with scaled start and scaled stride.
theorems.name: Scaling transports coil steps
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from distributivity and natural-number multiplication normalization inside ZMod.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {p k : Nat}, Nat.Prime p -> 0 < k -> k < p -> Function.Bijective (Circle.scale p k)
theorems.informal_statement: Every nonzero multiplier below a prime circle size gives a reversible scaling map.
theorems.name: Prime scaling is bijective
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0008 and Nat.coprime_of_lt_prime.

python_property_tests: for prime p and 1<=k<p: scale(p,k) is a permutation

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, k divides n -> Circle.scale n k ((n/k : Nat) : Circle.C n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: If k divides n, then scaling the cofactor address n/k by k lands at zero in C_n.
theorems.name: Scaling sends a divisor cofactor to zero
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean using Nat.mul_div_cancel' and the ZMod characteristic cast-to-zero fact.

python_property_tests: if k divides n and n>0, then scale(n,k,n//k)==0

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k m : Nat}, k divides n -> Circle.scale n k ((m*(n/k) : Nat) : Circle.C n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: If k divides n, then every multiple of the cofactor n/k lands at zero under scaling by k in C_n.
theorems.name: Scaling sends cofactor multiples to zero
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by rewriting k*(m*(n/k)) to m*n and casting the modulus multiple to zero in ZMod.

python_property_tests: if k divides n and n>0, then for m in a bounded range scale(n,k,m*(n//k))==0

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x m : Nat}, k divides n -> Circle.scale n k (((x + m*(n/k)) : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n)
theorems.informal_statement: If k divides n, then adding any multiple of n/k to an address does not change its scaled value under k.
theorems.name: Scaling collapses cofactor-shifted addresses
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0023, distributivity of multiplication over addition, and additive zero simplification in ZMod.

python_property_tests: if k divides n and n>0, then scale(n,k,x+m*(n//k))==scale(n,k,x) over bounded examples

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n k x : Nat), Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = 0 iff n divides k*x
theorems.informal_statement: A natural address x maps to zero under scaling by k exactly when n divides k*x.
theorems.name: Scaling zero iff modulus divides product
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by reducing scale to the cast of k*x in ZMod and applying CharP.cast_eq_zero_iff.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==0 iff (k*x)%n==0 over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n k x y : Nat), Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((y : Nat) : Circle.C n) iff k*x is congruent to k*y mod n
theorems.informal_statement: Two natural addresses have equal scaled values exactly when their scaled products are congruent modulo n.
theorems.name: Scaling equality iff scaled products are congruent
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by rewriting both scaled natural addresses as casts of k*x and k*y, then applying ZMod.natCast_eq_natCast_iff.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==scale(n,k,y) iff (k*x)%n==(k*y)%n over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x y : Nat}, Nat.Coprime n k -> Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((y : Nat) : Circle.C n) iff x is congruent to y mod n
theorems.informal_statement: When k is coprime to n, two natural addresses have equal scaled values exactly when the original addresses are congruent modulo n.
theorems.name: Coprime scaling reflects address congruence
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0008, injectivity of the bijective scaling map, and ZMod.natCast_eq_natCast_iff.

python_property_tests: if gcd(n,k)==1, then scale(n,k,x)==scale(n,k,y) iff x%n==y%n over bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n : Nat) (x : Circle.C n), Circle.scale n 0 x = 0
theorems.informal_statement: Scaling by zero sends every finite-circle address to zero.
theorems.name: Scaling by zero collapses to zero
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by reducing scale-by-zero to zero multiplication in ZMod.

python_property_tests: scale(n,0,x)==0 over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k l : Nat}, k congruent to l mod n -> forall x : Circle.C n, Circle.scale n k x = Circle.scale n l x
theorems.informal_statement: Scale factors that are congruent modulo n define the same scaling map on C_n.
theorems.name: Congruent scale factors act identically
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by casting scale factors into ZMod and applying ZMod.natCast_eq_natCast_iff.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==scale(n,k+m*n,x) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = 0 iff Circle.period n k divides x
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, a natural address scales to zero exactly when the stride period divides that address.
theorems.name: Scaling kernel is period divisibility
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0025, CC-T0005, gcd reduction, and coprime divisibility cancellation.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==0 iff x%period(n,k)==0 over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n k steps : Nat), Circle.scale n k ((steps : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.coilStep n k 0 steps
theorems.informal_statement: Scaling a natural step index by stride k gives the same address as walking k for that many coil steps from zero.
theorems.name: Scaling a step index gives the matching coil step
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by unfolding scale and coilStep and normalizing natural multiplication in ZMod.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,steps)==coil_step(n,k,0,steps) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k m : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scale n k ((m * Circle.period n k : Nat) : Circle.C n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, every natural multiple of the stride period maps to zero under scaling by that stride.
theorems.name: Scaling period multiples gives zero
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0030 and divisibility of a multiple by its factor.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,m*period(n,k))==0 over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x m : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scale n k (((x + m * Circle.period n k) : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n)
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, adding any natural multiple of the stride period to an address does not change its scaled value.
theorems.name: Scaling collapses period-shifted addresses
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0032, CC-T0025, and modular addition congruence.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x+m*period(n,k))==scale(n,k,x) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k (((x % Circle.period n k) : Nat) : Circle.C n)
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, scaling a natural address has the same value as scaling its residue modulo the stride period.
theorems.name: Scaling has period normal form
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0033 and Nat.mod_add_div.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==scale(n,k,x%period(n,k)) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x y : Nat}, n != 0 -> (Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((y : Nat) : Circle.C n) <-> x ≡ y [MOD Circle.period n k])
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, two natural addresses have the same scaled value exactly when they are congruent modulo the stride period.
theorems.name: Scaling equality is period congruence
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by reducing scaled equality to product congruence, factoring out gcd(n,k), and canceling the coprime reduced multiplier.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==scale(n,k,y) iff x%period(n,k)==y%period(n,k) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x y : Nat}, n != 0 -> x < Circle.period n k -> y < Circle.period n k -> (Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((y : Nat) : Circle.C n) <-> x = y)
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, scaling is injective on the natural representatives strictly below the stride period.
theorems.name: Scaling is injective on period representatives
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0035 and Nat.ModEq.eq_of_lt_of_lt.

python_property_tests: for 0<=x,y<period(n,k), scale(n,k,x)==scale(n,k,y) iff x==y over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> (Circle.scalePeriodRepresentativeImage n k).card = Circle.period n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the finite image of one full set of stride-period representatives under scaling has cardinality equal to the stride period.
theorems.name: Scaling period-representative image has period cardinality
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0036 and Finset.card_image_of_injective over Fin (period n k).

python_property_tests: len({scale(n,k,r) | 0 <= r < period(n,k)}) == period(n,k) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scale n k ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) in Circle.scalePeriodRepresentativeImage n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, every scaled natural address lies in the scaled image of one full set of stride-period representatives.
theorems.name: Scaled addresses land in period-representative image
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0034 by choosing the Fin representative x % period(n,k).

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x) is in {scale(n,k,r) | 0 <= r < period(n,k)} over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scaleCircleImage n k = Circle.scalePeriodRepresentativeImage n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the image of scaling all finite circle representatives equals the image of scaling one full set of stride-period representatives.
theorems.name: Scaling circle image equals period-representative image
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0038 and the bound period(n,k) <= n for nonzero n.

python_property_tests: {scale(n,k,x) | 0 <= x < n} == {scale(n,k,r) | 0 <= r < period(n,k)} over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> (Circle.scaleCircleImage n k).card = Circle.period n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the finite image of scaling all circle representatives has cardinality equal to the stride period.
theorems.name: Scaling circle image has period cardinality
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0039 and CC-T0037.

python_property_tests: len({scale(n,k,x) | 0 <= x < n}) == period(n,k) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scaleKernelRepresentativeSet n k = Circle.scalePeriodKernelRepresentatives n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the canonical representatives that map to zero under scaling are exactly the multiples of the stride period indexed below gcd(n,k).
theorems.name: Scaling kernel representatives are period multiples
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0030 and the factorization n = gcd(n,k) * period(n,k).

python_property_tests: {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=0} == {m*period(n,k) | 0 <= m < gcd(n,k)} over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> (Circle.scaleKernelRepresentativeSet n k).card = Nat.gcd n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the finite set of canonical representatives that map to zero under scaling has cardinality gcd(n,k).
theorems.name: Scaling kernel representative set has gcd cardinality
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0041 and injective enumeration of period multiples below gcd(n,k).

python_property_tests: len({x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=0}) == gcd(n,k) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k r : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k r = Circle.scalePeriodFiberRepresentatives n k r
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the canonical representatives in the scaled fiber of a representative r are exactly r modulo the stride period plus all period multiples indexed below gcd(n,k).
theorems.name: Scaling fibers are period-offset representatives
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0035, period normal form, and n = gcd(n,k) * period(n,k).

python_property_tests: {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=scale(n,k,r)} == {r % period(n,k) + m*period(n,k) | 0 <= m < gcd(n,k)} over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k r : Nat}, n != 0 -> (Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k r).card = Nat.gcd n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, every finite canonical representative fiber of the scaling map has cardinality gcd(n,k).
theorems.name: Scaling fibers have gcd cardinality
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0043 and injective enumeration of period-offset representatives.

python_property_tests: len({x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=scale(n,k,r)}) == gcd(n,k) over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k x : Nat}, n != 0 -> ((x : Nat) : Circle.C n) in Circle.scaleKernelSubgroup n k iff Circle.period n k divides x
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, a natural representative lies in the additive kernel subgroup of scaling by k exactly when the stride period divides that representative.
theorems.name: Scaling kernel subgroup membership is period divisibility
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by defining scaling as an additive hom, taking its kernel subgroup, and reusing CC-T0030.

python_property_tests: scale(n,k,x)==0 iff period(n,k) divides x over positive moduli in bounded tests

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat} {target : Circle.C n}, target notin Circle.scaleCircleImage n k -> Circle.scaleTargetFiberRepresentativeSet n k target = empty
theorems.informal_statement: For any target node not in the finite image of the scaling map, the canonical representative fiber over that target is empty.
theorems.name: Scaling target fibers are empty outside the image
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by contradiction: any representative in the target fiber would witness target membership in scaleCircleImage.

python_property_tests: if target notin {scale(n,k,x) | 0 <= x < n}, then {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=target} is empty

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> forall {target : Circle.C n}, target in Circle.scaleCircleImage n k -> (Circle.scaleTargetFiberRepresentativeSet n k target).card = Nat.gcd n k
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, any target node in the finite image of scaling has a canonical representative fiber of cardinality gcd(n,k).
theorems.name: Scaling target fibers inside the image have gcd cardinality
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by choosing an image representative and applying CC-T0044.

python_property_tests: if target in {scale(n,k,x) | 0 <= x < n}, then len({x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=target}) == gcd(n,k)

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> forall {target : Circle.C n}, target in Circle.scaleCircleImage n k -> (Circle.scaleCircleImage n k).card * (Circle.scaleTargetFiberRepresentativeSet n k target).card = n
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, any target node in the finite scaling image has image-cardinality times target-fiber-cardinality equal to the circle size.
theorems.name: Scaling image and target fiber cardinalities factor the circle
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0040, CC-T0047, and the factorization n = gcd(n,k) * period(n,k).

python_property_tests: if target in {scale(n,k,x) | 0 <= x < n}, then len(image) * len(target_fiber) == n

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> (Circle.scaleCircleImage n k).card * (Circle.scaleKernelRepresentativeSet n k).card = n
theorems.informal_statement: For a nonzero circle size, the finite scaling image cardinality times the canonical kernel representative cardinality equals the circle size.
theorems.name: Scaling image and kernel cardinalities factor the circle
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0040, CC-T0042, and the factorization n = gcd(n,k) * period(n,k).

python_property_tests: len({scale(n,k,x) | 0 <= x < n}) * len({x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=0}) == n

theorems.formal_statement: forall n k : Nat, Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k 0 = Circle.scaleKernelRepresentativeSet n k
theorems.informal_statement: For every finite circle and scaling factor, the representative fiber over the scaled zero address is exactly the canonical kernel representative set.
theorems.name: Scaling zero fiber is the kernel representative set
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by unfolding the two representative-set definitions and simplifying scale at zero.

python_property_tests: {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=scale(n,k,0)} == {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=0}

theorems.formal_statement: forall n k r : Nat, Circle.scaleTargetFiberRepresentativeSet n k (Circle.scale n k ((r : Nat) : Circle.C n)) = Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k r
theorems.informal_statement: For every finite circle, scaling factor, and representative r, the target fiber over scale(r) is exactly the canonical representative fiber of r.
theorems.name: Scaling target fiber over a scaled representative is its representative fiber
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by definitional equality of the target-fiber and representative-fiber filters.

python_property_tests: {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=target} == {x | 0 <= x < n and scale(n,k,x)=scale(n,k,r)} for target=scale(n,k,r)

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k r s : Nat}, r < n -> (Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k r = Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k s iff Circle.scale n k ((r : Nat) : Circle.C n) = Circle.scale n k ((s : Nat) : Circle.C n))
theorems.informal_statement: For a canonical representative r below n, its scaling fiber equals the fiber of s exactly when scale(r) equals scale(s).
theorems.name: Scaling representative fibers are equal iff scaled representatives are equal
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean by using membership of the canonical representative r in its own fiber and by extensional equality of the filters.

python_property_tests: for 0 <= r < n, fiber(r)==fiber(s) iff scale(n,k,r)==scale(n,k,s)

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k r s : Nat}, n != 0 -> r < n -> (Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k r = Circle.scaleFiberRepresentativeSet n k s iff r congruent s modulo Circle.period n k)
theorems.informal_statement: For nonzero n and a canonical representative r below n, its scaling fiber equals the fiber of s exactly when r and s are congruent modulo period(n,k).
theorems.name: Scaling representative fibers are equal iff representatives are period-congruent
theorems.proof_notes: Lean theorem is compiled in Circle/Core/Scaling.lean from CC-T0052 and the period-congruence equality theorem CC-T0035.

python_property_tests: for n > 0 and 0 <= r < n, fiber(r)==fiber(s) iff r % period(n,k) == s % period(n,k)

theorems.formal_statement: def eulerCharacteristic : List Nat -> Int, with [] = 0 and count :: rest = count - eulerCharacteristic rest
theorems.informal_statement: The Euler characteristic of a finite cell-count list is the alternating sum of its cell counts.

theorems.formal_statement: def suspensionCounts : List Nat -> List Nat, with [] = [2] and count :: rest = (count + 2) :: suspensionTail count rest
theorems.informal_statement: Suspension transforms finite cell counts by adding two poles and coning adjacent dimensions.

theorems.formal_statement: forall counts : List Nat, eulerCharacteristic (suspensionCounts counts) = 2 - eulerCharacteristic counts
theorems.informal_statement: Suspension changes Euler characteristic by chi(Susp K)=2-chi(K).

theorems.formal_statement: forall dimension : Nat, bottClockIndex dimension < 8
theorems.informal_statement: The finite period-8 dimension clock always returns an index below 8.

theorems.formal_statement: forall dimension : Nat, bottClockIndex (dimension + 8) = bottClockIndex dimension
theorems.informal_statement: Adding eight dimensions preserves the finite period-8 clock index.

theorems.formal_statement: bottClockIndex 0 = 0
theorems.informal_statement: Dimension zero has period-8 clock index zero.

theorems.formal_statement: forall interval : DirectedInterval, pointBoundary (intervalBoundary interval) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: In the directed-interval seed, taking point-boundary after interval-boundary gives zero.

theorems.formal_statement: forall interval : DirectedInterval, intervalBoundary (reverseInterval interval) = -intervalBoundary interval
theorems.informal_statement: Reversing a directed interval negates its boundary.

theorems.formal_statement: forall interval : DirectedInterval, reverseInterval (reverseInterval interval) = interval
theorems.informal_statement: Reversing a directed interval twice returns the original interval.

theorems.formal_statement: forall point : Int, intervalBoundary (constantInterval point) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: A constant directed interval has zero boundary.

theorems.formal_statement: forall base fiber, trivialBundleProjection (trivialBundlePoint base fiber) = base
theorems.informal_statement: In the trivial product-bundle seed, projecting a built point returns its base coordinate.

theorems.formal_statement: forall base fiber, trivialBundleFiber (trivialBundlePoint base fiber) = fiber
theorems.informal_statement: In the trivial product-bundle seed, reading a built point's fiber coordinate returns its fiber value.

theorems.formal_statement: forall base left right, trivialBundleProjection (trivialBundlePoint base left) = trivialBundleProjection (trivialBundlePoint base right)
theorems.informal_statement: In the trivial product-bundle seed, changing only the fiber value does not change the projected base.

theorems.formal_statement: forall left right fiber, trivialBundleFiber (trivialBundlePoint left fiber) = trivialBundleFiber (trivialBundlePoint right fiber)
theorems.informal_statement: In the trivial product-bundle seed, changing only the base value does not change the fiber coordinate.

theorems.formal_statement: forall glyphId theoremId leanName : String, proofGlyphTheoremId (proofGlyph glyphId theoremId leanName) = theoremId
theorems.informal_statement: A constructed proof glyph exposes the theorem id it carries.

theorems.formal_statement: forall glyphId theoremId leanName : String, proofGlyphLeanName (proofGlyph glyphId theoremId leanName) = leanName
theorems.informal_statement: A constructed proof glyph exposes the Lean declaration name it carries.

theorems.formal_statement: forall glyphId theoremId leanName : String, proofGlyphGlyphId (proofGlyph glyphId theoremId leanName) = glyphId
theorems.informal_statement: A constructed proof glyph exposes the glyph id it carries.

theorems.formal_statement: forall counts : List Nat, eulerCharacteristic (doubleSuspensionCounts counts) = eulerCharacteristic counts
theorems.informal_statement: Applying the finite suspension cell-count transform twice preserves Euler characteristic.

theorems.formal_statement: forall counts : List Nat, eulerCharacteristic (fourSuspensionCounts counts) = eulerCharacteristic counts
theorems.informal_statement: Four finite suspensions preserve Euler characteristic as two double-suspension steps.

theorems.formal_statement: forall counts : List Nat, fourSuspensionCounts counts = doubleSuspensionCounts (doubleSuspensionCounts counts)
theorems.informal_statement: The finite four-suspension count operation is two double-suspension steps.

theorems.formal_statement: Nat.card Circle.S0.Sign = 2
theorems.informal_statement: The S^0 opposition model has exactly two points.

theorems.formal_statement: forall x : Circle.S0.Sign, Circle.S0.antipode (Circle.S0.antipode x) = x
theorems.informal_statement: The antipodal map on S^0 has order two.

theorems.formal_statement: forall x : Circle.S0.Sign, Circle.S0.antipode x != x
theorems.informal_statement: The antipodal map on S^0 has no fixed point.

theorems.formal_statement: Circle.S0.antipode Circle.S0.Sign.neg = Circle.S0.Sign.pos and Circle.S0.antipode Circle.S0.Sign.pos = Circle.S0.Sign.neg
theorems.informal_statement: The antipodal map on S^0 swaps the two named signs.

theorems.blocker: Roadmap marker only. It is not a proof of the full octonionic Hopf fibration.
theorems.formal_statement: def octonionicHopfRoadmap : String := "S7 -> S15 -> S8"
theorems.informal_statement: The future octonionic Hopf horizon has intended structure S^7 -> S^15 -> S^8.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.Future.S15.topologicalModel n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: The finite topological S15 suspension model has Euler characteristic 0.

theorems.formal_statement: forall p : Circle.Future.S15.OctonionPair, Circle.Future.S15.octonionPairNormSq p = 1 -> Circle.Future.S15.hopfBase9NormSq (Circle.Future.S15.octonionicHopfMap p) = 1
theorems.informal_statement: The bounded octonionic Hopf coordinate map sends normalized octonion pairs to the unit nine-coordinate base equation.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Future.S15.topologicalModel n = Circle.Future.S15.eightSuspensions (Circle.S7.iteratedSuspensionModel n)
theorems.informal_statement: The finite S15 topological model is the eightfold suspension of the finite S7 model.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall x : Circle.C n, Circle.S1.signedRot n 0 x = x
theorems.informal_statement: Signed rotation by zero leaves every finite-circle node unchanged.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall a b : Int, forall x : Circle.C n, Circle.S1.signedRot n a (Circle.S1.signedRot n b x) = Circle.S1.signedRot n (a+b) x
theorems.informal_statement: Composing signed rotations adds their signed strides.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall k : Int, forall x : Circle.C n, Circle.S1.signedRot n (-k) (Circle.S1.signedRot n k x) = x
theorems.informal_statement: A signed rotation followed by the opposite signed rotation returns to the starting node.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.S2.suspendedCircleCounts n = [n+2, 3*n, 2*n]
theorems.informal_statement: For n>=3, the suspended finite circle has V=n+2, E=3n, and F=2n.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S2.suspendedCircleCounts n) = 2
theorems.informal_statement: For n>=3, the suspended finite circle has Euler characteristic 2.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n r : Nat, Circle.S2.sphereGridCounts n r = [n*r+2, n*r+n*(r+1), n*(r+1)]
theorems.informal_statement: For n>=3 and r>=1, SphereGrid(n,r) has V=nr+2, E=n(2r+1), and F=n(r+1).

theorems.formal_statement: forall n r : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S2.sphereGridCounts n r) = 2
theorems.informal_statement: For n>=3 and r>=1, SphereGrid(n,r) has Euler characteristic 2.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (n r : Nat) (latitude : Fin r), Circle.S2.LatitudeRing n r latitude = Circle.C n
theorems.informal_statement: Every non-pole latitude ring in a sphere grid is C_n-like.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n r stride : Nat, longitudeRotation n r stride north = north and longitudeRotation n r stride south = south
theorems.informal_statement: A latitude rotation acts on ring nodes and fixes the collapsed poles.

theorems.formal_statement: forall {n k : Nat}, n != 0 -> Circle.period n k = n / Nat.gcd n k
theorems.informal_statement: A fixed latitude ring inherits the S1 coil period n/gcd(n,k).

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, suspendedCircleAntipode n north = south and suspendedCircleAntipode n south = north
theorems.informal_statement: The suspended-circle antipode swaps the north and south poles.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall point : Circle.S2.SuspendedCirclePoint n, Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipode n (Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipode n point) = point
theorems.informal_statement: Applying the suspended-circle antipode twice returns the original point.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall point : Circle.S2.SuspendedCirclePoint n, Circle.S2.isSuspendedPole (Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipode n point) iff Circle.S2.isSuspendedPole point
theorems.informal_statement: The suspended-circle antipode preserves the finite pole subset.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall point : Circle.S2.SuspendedCirclePoint n, Circle.S2.isSuspendedEquator (Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipode n point) iff Circle.S2.isSuspendedEquator point
theorems.informal_statement: The suspended-circle antipode preserves the finite equator subset.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n r stride : Nat, forall point : Circle.S2.SphereGridPoint n r, Circle.S2.sphereGridLatitudeCoordinate (Circle.S2.longitudeRotation n r stride point) = Circle.S2.sphereGridLatitudeCoordinate point
theorems.informal_statement: Longitude rotation preserves the finite latitude coordinate of every sphere-grid point.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n r stride : Nat, forall point : Circle.S2.SphereGridPoint n r, Circle.S2.sphereGridLongitudeCoordinate (Circle.S2.longitudeRotation n r stride point) = Option.map (Circle.rot n stride) (Circle.S2.sphereGridLongitudeCoordinate point)
theorems.informal_statement: Longitude rotation advances the finite longitude coordinate by the corresponding circle rotation while leaving poles without a longitude coordinate.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall point : Circle.S2.SuspendedCirclePoint n, Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipodalPair n point (Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipode n point)
theorems.informal_statement: Every suspended-circle point forms a finite antipodal pair with its antipode.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, forall p q : Circle.S2.SuspendedCirclePoint n, Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipodalPair n p q iff Circle.S2.suspendedCircleAntipodalPair n q p
theorems.informal_statement: The finite suspended-circle antipodal-pair relation is symmetric.

theorems.formal_statement: forall vertices edges faces : Nat, Circle.S3.suspensionSurfaceCounts vertices edges faces = [vertices+2, edges+2*vertices, faces+2*edges, 2*faces]
theorems.informal_statement: Suspending a finite surface count model transforms V,E,F into V+2,E+2V,F+2E,2F.

theorems.formal_statement: forall vertices edges faces : Nat, eulerCharacteristic [vertices,edges,faces] = 2 -> eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S3.suspensionSurfaceCounts vertices edges faces) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: If a finite surface model has Euler characteristic 2, its suspension has Euler characteristic 0.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.S3.suspendedSuspendedCircleCounts n = [n+4, 5*n+4, 8*n, 4*n]
theorems.informal_statement: Susp(SuspC(n)) has V=n+4, E=5n+4, F=8n, and T=4n.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S3.suspendedSuspendedCircleCounts n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: Susp(SuspC(n)) has Euler characteristic 0.

theorems.formal_statement: forall p : Circle.S3.HopfPair, Circle.S3.hopfPairNormSq p = 1 -> Circle.S3.hopfBaseNormSq (Circle.S3.hopfMap p) = 1
theorems.informal_statement: The coordinate Hopf map sends normalized S^3 pairs to the unit S^2 equation.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (u v : ℝ) (p : Circle.S3.HopfPair), u*u+v*v=1 -> Circle.S3.hopfMap (Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair u v p) = Circle.S3.hopfMap p
theorems.informal_statement: Simultaneous unit phase rotation preserves the Hopf map.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (u v : ℝ) (p : Circle.S3.HopfPair), u*u+v*v=1 -> Circle.S3.hopfPairNormSq p = 1 -> Circle.S3.hopfPairNormSq (Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair u v p) = 1 and Circle.S3.hopfMap (Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair u v p) = Circle.S3.hopfMap p
theorems.informal_statement: A unit phase orbit stays on the normalized S3 pair space and preserves the Hopf base point.

theorems.formal_statement: forall p : Circle.S3.HopfPair, Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair 1 0 p = p
theorems.informal_statement: The identity phase leaves every Hopf pair unchanged.

theorems.formal_statement: forall u v a b : Real, forall p : Circle.S3.HopfPair, Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair u v (Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair a b p) = Circle.S3.phaseRotatePair (u*a-v*b) (v*a+u*b) p
theorems.informal_statement: Composing two Hopf phase rotations follows the complex phase multiplication law.

theorems.formal_statement: def quaternionNorm (q : Circle.S3.RealQuaternion) : Real := Quaternion.normSq q
theorems.informal_statement: Define the quaternion norm used for the unit-quaternion S^3 model.

theorems.formal_statement: forall p q : Circle.S3.UnitQuaternion, Circle.S3.quaternionNorm (p.val * q.val) = 1
theorems.informal_statement: Unit quaternions are closed under multiplication.

theorems.formal_statement: forall q : Circle.S3.UnitQuaternion, q.val * star q.val = 1 and star q.val * q.val = 1
theorems.informal_statement: The inverse of a unit quaternion is its conjugate.

theorems.formal_statement: Circle.S3.quaternionI * Circle.S3.quaternionJ != Circle.S3.quaternionJ * Circle.S3.quaternionI
theorems.informal_statement: Quaternion multiplication is noncommutative by a formal example.

theorems.formal_statement: forall a b c : Circle.S3.RealQuaternion, (a*b)*c = a*(b*c)
theorems.informal_statement: Quaternion multiplication is associative.

theorems.formal_statement: forall q v : Circle.S3.RealQuaternion, Circle.S3.quaternionConjugationAction (-q) v = Circle.S3.quaternionConjugationAction q v
theorems.informal_statement: The quaternion conjugation action is unchanged by replacing q with -q.

theorems.formal_statement: forall v : Circle.S3.RealQuaternion, Circle.S3.quaternionConjugationAction 1 v = v
theorems.informal_statement: The identity quaternion conjugation action fixes every input quaternion.

theorems.formal_statement: forall q : Circle.S3.RealQuaternion, Circle.S3.quaternionConjugationAction q 0 = 0
theorems.informal_statement: Quaternion conjugation sends the zero input quaternion to zero.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S4.counts n) = 2
theorems.informal_statement: The finite iterated-suspension model of S^4 has Euler characteristic 2.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.S4.counts n = Circle.Common.suspensionCounts (Circle.S3.suspendedSuspendedCircleCounts n)
theorems.informal_statement: The finite S4 counts are defined as the suspension of the finite S3 counts.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, chi(S4(n)) = 2 and chi(S5(n)) = 0 and chi(S6(n)) = 2 for the iterated finite suspension models
theorems.informal_statement: The finite iterated-suspension model satisfies chi(S^d)=1+(-1)^d.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S5.counts n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: The finite iterated-suspension model of S^5 has Euler characteristic 0.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.S5.counts n = Circle.Common.suspensionCounts (Circle.S4.counts n)
theorems.informal_statement: The finite S5 counts are defined as the suspension of the finite S4 counts.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S6.counts n) = 2
theorems.informal_statement: The finite iterated-suspension model of S^6 has Euler characteristic 2.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.S6.counts n = Circle.Common.suspensionCounts (Circle.S5.counts n)
theorems.informal_statement: The finite S6 counts are defined as the suspension of the finite S5 counts.

theorems.formal_statement: def iteratedSuspensionModel (n : Nat) : List Nat := Circle.Common.suspensionCounts (Circle.S6.counts n)
theorems.informal_statement: S^7 is constructed as a finite iterated-suspension model.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.Common.eulerCharacteristic (Circle.S7.iteratedSuspensionModel n) = 0
theorems.informal_statement: The finite iterated-suspension model of S^7 has Euler characteristic 0.

theorems.formal_statement: forall n : Nat, Circle.S7.iteratedSuspensionModel n = Circle.Common.suspensionCounts (Circle.S6.counts n)
theorems.informal_statement: The finite S7 iterated-suspension model is defined as the suspension of the finite S6 counts.

theorems.formal_statement: forall idx : Fin 8, Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord idx) = 1
theorems.informal_statement: Each coordinate octonion basis element has squared norm one.

theorems.formal_statement: forall o : Circle.S7.OctonionCoord, Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord o (Circle.S7.octonionConjCoord o) = Circle.S7.octonionRealCoord (Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq o)
theorems.informal_statement: Define octonion conjugate and norm.

theorems.formal_statement: forall a b : Circle.S7.OctonionCoord, Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq (Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord a b) = Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq a * Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq b
theorems.informal_statement: Octonion norm is multiplicative.

theorems.formal_statement: forall a b : Circle.S7.OctonionCoord, Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq a = 1 -> Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq b = 1 -> Circle.S7.octonionCoordNormSq (Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord a b) = 1
theorems.informal_statement: Unit octonions are closed under multiplication, without forming a group.

theorems.formal_statement: Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨1, by norm_num⟩) (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨2, by norm_num⟩) != Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨2, by norm_num⟩) (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨1, by norm_num⟩)
theorems.informal_statement: Octonion multiplication is noncommutative by a formal example.

theorems.formal_statement: Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord (Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨1, by norm_num⟩) (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨2, by norm_num⟩)) (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨4, by norm_num⟩) != Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨1, by norm_num⟩) (Circle.S7.octonionMulCoord (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨2, by norm_num⟩) (Circle.S7.octonionBasisCoord ⟨4, by norm_num⟩))
theorems.informal_statement: Octonion multiplication is nonassociative by a formal example.

theorems.formal_statement: forall p : Circle.S7.QuaternionPair, Circle.S7.quaternionPairNormSq p = 1 -> Circle.S7.hopfBase5NormSq (Circle.S7.quaternionicHopfMap p) = 1
theorems.informal_statement: The quaternionic Hopf coordinate map sends normalized quaternion pairs to the unit S4 equation.

theorems.formal_statement: forall (p : Circle.S7.QuaternionPair) (u : Circle.S7.QuaternionCoord), Circle.S7.quaternionCoordNormSq u = 1 -> Circle.S7.quaternionicHopfMap (Circle.S7.quaternionPairRightPhase p u) = Circle.S7.quaternionicHopfMap p
theorems.informal_statement: Quaternionic phase invariance is the S^7 analog of Hopf phase invariance.
